result:
Most mornings I wake before sunrise, guided not by an alarm but by the steady drip of a kitchen faucet that stubbornly refuses to be silent. In that half-awake state, I lie there imagining the tiny droplets as tiny percussionists, practicing syncopated rhythms somewhere between jazz and drizzle. Eventually curiosity wins, and I shuffle toward the window to see whether the world has changed overnight. Usually it hasn’t, yet there is comfort in confirming the persistence of sidewalks, lamp posts, and the neighbor’s audacious garden gnome. He stands there, mid-wink, as if guarding secrets about mail delivery and pollen forecasts.

Coffee is my first negotiation with the day. I promise productivity, it provides motivation, and together we attempt diplomacy with a to-do list that keeps multiplying like enthusiastic rabbits. Between emails, I invent elaborate backstories for the people mentioned in the news. The meteorologist becomes a time traveler. The mayor moonlights as a street poet. Even the weather map adopts personality—storm fronts shaped like sleeping dragons, high-pressure systems stretching like cats.

When fatigue threatens a coup, I retreat into music. A single violin can carry more honesty than several committee meetings combined. Its notes search rooms the mind forgot, brushing dust off memories and letting them breathe a little before returning to quiet shelves. Occasionally I answer messages, occasionally I don’t; but I always try to end the day with a walk. The faucet will surely start the next dawn, keeping tempo for the ordinary symphony I stubbornly adore.

Most mornings I wake before sunrise, guided not by an alarm but by the steady drip of a kitchen faucet that stubbornly refuses to be silent. In that half-awake state, I lie there imagining the tiny droplets as tiny percussionists, practicing syncopated rhythms somewhere between jazz and drizzle. Eventually curiosity wins, and I shuffle toward the window to see whether the world has changed overnight. Usually it hasn’t, yet there is comfort in confirming the persistence of sidewalks, lamp posts, and the neighbor’s audacious garden gnome. He stands there, mid-wink, as if guarding secrets about mail delivery and pollen forecasts.

Coffee is my first negotiation with the day. I promise productivity, it provides motivation, and together we attempt diplomacy with a to-do list that keeps multiplying like enthusiastic rabbits. Between emails, I invent elaborate backstories for the people mentioned in the news. The meteorologist becomes a time traveler. The mayor moonlights as a street poet. Even the weather map adopts personality—storm fronts shaped like sleeping dragons, high-pressure systems stretching like cats.

When fatigue threatens a coup, I retreat into music. A single violin can carry more honesty than several committee meetings combined. Its notes search rooms the mind forgot, brushing dust off memories and letting them breathe a little before returning to quiet shelves. Occasionally I answer messages, occasionally I don’t; but I always try to end the day with a walk. The faucet will surely start the next dawn, keeping tempo for the ordinary symphony I stubbornly adore.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
